title: design-doc
description: Minimal design specification for one webdev-bench site. Produced by the author prompt (recipe/01-generate/prompts/author.md), consumed by the builder. The grader does NOT read this — precompute extracts reference values from the rendered ground-truth.

description.description: One-line meaning of the website — what it is and what it represents. Example: 'Dashboard for fintech analytics tracking trading performance across portfolios'.

allowed_frameworks.description: Frameworks the agent is allowed to use. For this benchmark, always ['html-css']. The framework_compliance gate (×0.3) enforces this at grade time.

palette.description: Concrete color palette the builder must use. Carried in design.json so generated designs don't all converge on the same warm-cream default. Agent never sees this field (it's isolated to the verifier container); it's consumed by the builder only.

surface.description: Card / panel / sidebar background, distinct from page bg (~5-15% lightness offset).

text.description: Primary body text color. Must have ≥ 4.5:1 contrast against background.

accent.description: Primary brand color — buttons, links, active states. Use sparingly.

accent_alt.description: Optional secondary accent — a status color or complementary brand color.

muted.description: Optional secondary text / borders / disabled state.

name.description: Lowercase kebab-case; becomes <name>.html per the canonical-filename convention.

components.description: Three to five components, ordered top-to-bottom on the page. Each component is a single sentence (≤ 250 chars). Forces complexity to spread across pages, not pile up on one.

animations.description: Exactly one animation per page (Part 2 contract). The animation lives on a SMALL, bounded widget that sits ABOVE the fold at desktop 1440×900 — inside the hero or top component — so the user sees the entrance fire on first paint. Closed-enum taxonomy so the builder maps each type to a fixed @keyframes template.

element.description: One short phrase identifying the small animated widget AND naming where on the page it sits. The widget MUST be **above the desktop fold (y < 900)** — typically a hero CTA button, a kicker/eyebrow label above the hero headline, a hero status pill, a top-nav action button, or a primary first-section badge — so the on-load entrance is visible on first paint. Good examples: 'Primary Get started CTA button inside the hero block'; 'Eyebrow kicker label above the hero headline'; 'Status pill in the top navigation bar'. Bad examples (below the fold — DO NOT pick these): 'Footer trust strip'; 'Last review card'; 'Pagination bar at the bottom of the list'. The builder uses this to (a) place the small element inside the hero or top component, and (b) apply the wdvb-anim-<type>-<page-name> class to THAT element specifically.

type.description: Entrance animation class. Each starts off-screen / off-scale and translates or scales into final position, with opacity fading 0 → 1. slide-up: starts 120 px below + opacity 0 (rises into place). slide-down: starts 120 px above + opacity 0 (drops into place). slide-left: starts 120 px to the right + opacity 0 (comes from the right). slide-right: starts 120 px to the left + opacity 0 (comes from the left). scale-up: starts at scale(0.6) + opacity 0 (grows into place). Directional slides are 'hard to miss' because the element visibly traverses screen distance.

trigger.description: Load-triggered only (Part 2 contract). The animation fires once on page load — it is visible on first paint because the widget sits above the fold.

duration_ms.description: Animation duration in milliseconds. 1200–1500 ms is the sweet spot — long enough that the 120 px translation is unmissable, short enough not to feel sluggish on page load.

easing.description: CSS timing function. 'ease-out' is the default for entrances (fast at the start, decelerating into place). 'ease-in-out' fits scale-up well. Avoid 'linear' for entrances — it looks mechanical.
